title_en: Something Is Very Wrong with Modern Longevity Science
title_zh: 现代长寿科学有个严重问题

paragraphs.en: Something Is Very Wrong with Modern Longevity Science
paragraphs.zh: 现代长寿科学有个严重问题

paragraphs.en: A new book argues that many of the world’s oldest people aren’t so old after all.
paragraphs.zh: 一本新书认为，地球上许多“最长寿”的人其实并不像看起来那样长寿。

paragraphs.en: A fundamental challenge that longevity gurus face is that what’s true is often boring, and what’s interesting often isn’t true.Illustration by Armando Veve
paragraphs.zh: 长寿领域的一大基本难题在于：真正正确的往往很枯燥，而听起来有趣的往往并不真实。（插图：Armando Veve）

paragraphs.en: Jiroemon Kimura, who is on record as the world’s oldest man, died at the reported age of a hundred and sixteen, in 2013. His passing spawned a slew of articles about the secret to extreme longevity. (Apparently, it’s small meals.) Kimura is said to have been born in a fishing village in Kyoto Prefecture, Japan, in April or March of 1897. Strangely, he is the only one of his five siblings to have multiple graduation records; he seems to have completed elementary school in 1907, or 1909, or 1911. He supposedly married his wife on three different dates, and at some point he not only adopted his wife’s surname but also changed his first name. (He was born Kinjiro Miyake.) Demographers who attempted to validate his age wrote that their investigation uncovered irregularities and inconsistencies. And yet they concluded that “no critical discordances were discovered.” The word “critical” was doing a lot of work.
paragraphs.zh: 木村二郎（Jiroemon Kimura）是官方记录上世界上最年长的男性，2013年以据称一百一十六岁去世。他的去世引发了一系列关于“超高寿命秘诀”的报道。（有人说答案就是少吃。）据说，木村生于1897年4月或3月，日本京都府一个渔村。他的情况很怪：在他五个兄弟姐妹中，他是唯一留下多份毕业记录的人；据称他在1907年、1909年或1911年完成了小学教育。他似乎在三个不同日期与同一位妻子结婚过，并且在某个阶段不仅改用了妻子的姓，还改了名字（他的出生名是“宮見清次郎”，日语拼作 Kinjiro Miyake）。曾试图核实他年龄的统计人口研究人员在报告中写道，调查发现了许多不一致和矛盾之处。然而，他们最终还是得出结论："未发现关键性矛盾"。其中“关键性”一词在这句话里负担了很重的意义。

paragraphs.en: Cases such as Kimura’s—and some that inspire even more skepticism—fill the pages of “Morbid: Debunking Modern Longevity Science” (M.I.T. Press), a lively and sometimes conspiratorial new book by the Oxford researcher Saul Justin Newman. Newman won the satirical Ig Nobel Prize, in 2024, for demonstrating that many of the world’s oldest living people may actually be dead. In “Morbid,” he argues that dubious age claims are not isolated—blemishes on the record of, say, Jiroemon Kimura or the famed elder Frenchwoman Jeanne Calment—but, rather, a systemic problem infecting longevity studies. “It is pretty hard to publish a scientific case report about a grandma reaching 88 years old, but if she manages 122 you might even squeeze a book out of it,” Newman writes. An exceptionally long life, in Newman’s telling, is not necessarily a function of good genes, good behaviors, or good luck. It’s evidence of bad record-keeping. The world’s oldest people, he quips, have “birth certificate allergies.”
paragraphs.zh: 像木村（Kimura）这样的案例，以及一些更让人怀疑的案例，充满了《死亡边缘：拆解现代长寿科学》（Morbid: Debunking Modern Longevity Science，麻省理工学院出版社）这本新书的篇章。这本书由牛津大学研究员沙尔·贾斯汀·纽曼（Saul Justin Newman）撰写，既生动又时而带着阴谋色彩。纽曼在2024年因其“证明世界上许多最年长者其实可能已经去世”而获得了讽刺性的伊格诺贝尔奖。在《Morbid》中，他认为可疑的高龄宣称并非个案——比如木村仁右卫门（Jiroemon Kimura）或著名的法国老人夏安·卡尔芒（Jeanne Calment）的记录，也不是孤立污点，而是渗透在长寿研究中的系统性问题。纽曼写道：“一份关于奶奶活到88岁左右的科学病例报告并不难发表，但如果她能活到122岁，你甚至可能拿它写成一本书。”在纽曼看来，寿命特别长并不一定是基因好、行为好或运气好的体现，而是档案记录混乱的证据。他讽刺道，世界上最年长的人群患有“出生证明过敏症”。

paragraphs.en: In accordance with Newman’s theory, many long-lived people are hardly paragons of healthy choices. Christian Mortensen, who held the record for the world’s oldest man before Kimura, smoked for nearly a century. Juan Vicente Pérez—who was born in rural Venezuela, had no official papers until his fifties, and seemingly died at a hundred and fourteen—drank aguardiente every day. The Guinness Book of Records once credited Carrie White, who was given a diagnosis of “post-typhoid psychosis” in her thirties, with being the longest-lived person—until it learned of a clerical error at the psychiatric institution where she spent more than seven decades of her life. “Where are all the athletes?” Newman wonders. “The aerobically svelte, annoyingly pious, scare-you-with-their-abs types? Dead.” According to Newman, the reason that supercentenarians—people who live past a hundred and ten—frequently have unhealthy habits is that they aren’t supercentenarians at all.
paragraphs.zh: 按照纽曼的理论，许多高寿者几乎算不上健康行为的模范。卡尔文森（Christian Mortensen）——在木村之前曾持有世界最高龄男性的纪录——吸烟近百年。胡安·维森特·佩雷斯（Juan Vicente Pérez）出生在委内瑞拉乡村，直到五十多岁才拿到正式证件，表面上生于一百一十四岁去世，他每天都喝甘蔗蒸馏酒（aguardiente）。《吉尼斯世界纪录》曾把凯丽·怀特（Carrie White）列为最长寿的人，她在三十岁时被诊断为“伤寒后精神病”，直到后来说发现一处精神病院文书错误，而这家精神病院是她在其一生中度过了七十多年。纽曼追问：“那些运动员都去哪了？那些有氧训练、身材瘦削、还总是虔诚到让人烦人的、靠腹肌就能吓人的人呢？都死了。”纽曼认为，百岁十以上超高龄者（supercentenarian）之所以常有不健康习惯，是因为他们根本就不是超高龄者。

paragraphs.en: Discover notable new fiction, nonfiction, and poetry.
paragraphs.zh: 发现引人注目的新小说、非小说类作品和诗歌。

paragraphs.en: In a useful thought experiment, Newman shows how small errors in recorded age can compound to undermine aging research. Suppose that a hundred forty-year-olds fake their ages so that on official documents they’re listed as fifty. (The U.S. Census Bureau has said that millions of Americans misrepresented their age in the most recent census.) In a country that contains a hundred thousand fifty-year-olds, these “young fakers” would account for only 0.1 per cent of the population. No big deal. But because the faux fifty-year-olds are far less likely to die than real fifty-year-olds—after age thirty, our risk of death doubles roughly every eight years—they will come to make up a larger and larger share of their purported age group. At “sixty-six,” they represent 0.4 per cent of their cohort; at “eighty,” three per cent. At “a hundred and five,” they compose virtually the entire population.
paragraphs.zh: 在一个有用的思想实验中，纽曼展示了年龄记录中的细小错误如何逐步放大，进而削弱衰老研究。假设有一百名报假年龄的人把自己伪报为50岁（按官方文件显示）。（美国人口普查局表示，最近一次人口普查中有数百万美国人年龄报得不实。）在一个有十万名真实50岁人的国家里，这些“假50岁者”仅占0.1%。这不算大问题。但因为这些假50岁者的死亡率远低于真实50岁者——30岁后，死亡风险约每八年翻一倍——他们会在所宣称的年龄组里占比不断上升。在“66岁”时，他们占该组的0.4%；在“80岁”时，占3%。到“105岁”时，他们几乎构成了整个“群体”。

paragraphs.en: This is a problem for longevity science, and also for public policy. All sorts of profound decisions depend on how long we’re expected to live: the amount we pay into both Medicare and Social Security and the rate at which we’re paid back, the cost of insurance, the funding that’s allocated to various medical specialties and facilities, the amount of infrastructure that’s built for seniors. Unfortunately, such decisions are not always sound. In 2010, on Respect for the Aged Day, Japanese officials sought out a Tokyo man named Sogen Kato to congratulate him on living to a hundred and eleven. Instead, they found a mummified corpse in his bedroom. Kato had been dead for three decades, and his family had fraudulently collected more than a hundred thousand dollars in pension money. A government investigation later revealed that hundreds of thousands of “living” residents of Japan had in fact gone missing or died, including eighty per cent of Japanese centenarians. During the fiscal belt-tightening of the Great Recession, Greece discovered that two hundred thousand people—about two per cent of the population—were engaged in pension fraud. Most of the country’s supposed centenarians were a product of this deception, a government minister said; nearly three-quarters of them vanished in a subsequent census, conducted in 2011.
paragraphs.zh: 这对长寿科学来说是个问题，也会影响公共政策。许多重大决策都取决于我们预期寿命的长短：我们向医疗保障与社会保障系统缴纳的金额以及最终领取的比例、保险费率、分配给不同医疗专科和医疗机构的资金、为老年人建设多少基础设施。不幸的是，这些决策并不总是可靠的。2010年，在日本的“尊老日”当天，官员们去找一位名叫Sogen Kato的东京老人，想要祝贺他活到一百一十一岁。结果却在他卧室里发现了一具木乃伊般的尸体。Kato其实已经去世三十年，他的家人还非法领取了十几万美元以上的养老金。后来一项政府调查揭示，日本成百上千名“仍在世”的居民实际上失踪或死亡，其中包括八成日本百岁老人。希腊在大衰退期间的财政紧缩中发现，约有20万人——约占全国人口的2%——参与了养老金欺诈。该国一位部长说，大多数所谓的百岁老人都是这场骗局的产物；在2011年的一项后续人口普查中，其中近四分之三的人口消失了。

paragraphs.en: In the United States, centenarians are less common in generations for which birth certificates are widely available. But even government documents can deceive. The Inspector General of the Department of Health and Human Services has warned that people can easily obtain fakes; in 2010, Puerto Rico invalidated all its existing birth certificates, citing rampant fraud and identity theft. One study found that, in the middle of the twentieth century, more than a quarter of Americans had ages listed on census documents that were inconsistent with those on their death certificates. In some demographic groups, as many as two-thirds of the population had discrepancies. Some were large enough to classify individuals in an entirely different age bracket.
paragraphs.zh: 在美国，随着更广泛地保存出生证明，百岁老人的数量在较年轻的一代人中较少见。但即使是政府文件也可能具有欺骗性。美国卫生与公众服务部监察长曾警告说，人们很容易拿到假证件；2010年，波多黎各以“盗用身份和造假泛滥”为由作废了所有现有出生证明。一项研究发现，在20世纪中叶，超过四分之一美国人的年龄，在人口普查文件上与其死亡证明上的年龄不一致。在一些人口群体中，多达三分之二的人口都存在年龄不一致的情况。其中一些差异大到足以把个人归入完全不同的年龄段。

paragraphs.en: It’s jarring to think that our age—unlike our height or weight—can’t be precisely measured. One can count the rings on a tree to calculate how many years it was alive, but there’s no equivalent marker in humans. Every so often, in the hospital where I work, I notice that a patient has a listed age of more than a hundred and twenty. This is disorienting until I realize that the patient may have come into the hospital with no I.D. and no known contacts, and that they might be suffering from a condition—dementia, schizophrenia—that makes it hard for them to remember who they are, let alone how old. In such cases, the hospital’s computer system defaults to a birth year of 1900. Recently, tech companies have increasingly claimed that various products can reveal a person’s “biological age,” and scientists have made real progress toward developing “epigenetic clocks,” which analyze patterns in DNA to estimate the age of a person or an individual organ. But, so far, these approaches remain approximations, and they are less reliable in the very old.
paragraphs.zh: 想到年龄不像身高或体重一样可以精确测量，这件事令人不安。人可以数树木年轮来计算它活了多少年，但人类却没有可对应的标记。每隔一段时间，在我工作的医院里，我会注意到某位病人的登记年龄写得超过120岁。这会让我一阵错愕，直到我意识到该病人可能是没有身份证、也没有已知联系人来院，就诊时甚至无法记清自己的身份，更不用说年龄了，而且他们可能还患有痴呆症、精神分裂症等让人难以回忆自我身份的疾病。在这种情况下，医院的计算机系统默认使用1900年作为出生年份。最近，科技公司越来越多地宣称各种产品可以揭示一个人的“生物年龄”，科学家也在“表观遗传钟”方面取得了真正进展，通过分析DNA模式来估算一个人或单个器官的年龄。然而到目前为止，这些方法仍是近似估计，并且在高龄人群中更不可靠。

paragraphs.en: If Newman is correct that many of the oldest people are younger than we think, then our conclusions about them—and about the places that produce them—must be unreliable. His theory appears to explain some counterintuitive findings. An unusual number of centenarians seem to hail from low-income areas. The French Republic’s overseas départements of Guadeloupe and Martinique, which have some of its highest rates of poverty, also have France’s highest concentration of hundred-and-ten-year-olds. Newman’s own research suggests that London’s impoverished Tower Hamlets neighborhood has more hundred-and-five-year-olds than the rest of England combined.
paragraphs.zh: 如果纽曼的观点正确，也就是我们认为最年长的人其实可能比想象更年轻，那么我们关于这些人的结论，以及关于产生这些人的地区的结论，都可能不可靠。这个理论似乎能解释一些反直觉的发现。大量的百岁老人似乎来自低收入地区。法国本土海外省瓜德罗普岛和马提尼克岛——这些地区之一有法国最高的贫困率——却拥有法国最高浓度的一百一十岁以上老人。纽曼自己的研究还指出，伦敦的贫困地区塔伊姆利茨（Tower Hamlets）有比英格兰其他地区加起来更多的一百零五岁老人。

paragraphs.en: Newman aims his most pointed criticisms at so-called blue zones, places that are credited with producing extraordinary longevity. The term was introduced in a 2004 paper in which researchers tried to figure out why so many Sardinians lived past a hundred. The research team, led by a respected demographer named Michel Poulain, interviewed residents, checked documents, and recorded in blue ink on a map the places where centenarians lived. The blue clustered in a mountainous region in the middle of the island, leading the investigators to speculate about potential longevity-boosting factors, including the area’s climate and its inhabitants’ diet, life style, and high rate of inbreeding. The following year, a journalist named Dan Buettner published a hugely popular National Geographic cover story that introduced more longevity hot spots, including the Japanese prefecture of Okinawa, and Loma Linda, California.
paragraphs.zh: 纽曼对所谓“长寿地带”提出了最尖锐的批评。这一术语最初出现在一篇 2004 年的论文中，论文试图解释为何如此多的撒丁岛居民能活到一百岁以上。由著名人口统计学家米歇尔·普兰（Michel Poulain）领导的研究团队采访了居民，核对了文档，并在地图上用蓝色墨水标出百岁老人居住的地点。蓝点集中在岛中央的一个山区地区，于是研究人员开始猜测有哪些可能延长寿命的因素，包括当地气候、居民饮食和生活方式、以及较高的近亲繁殖率。次年，记者丹·布特纳（Dan Buettner）在《国家地理》发表了一篇极受欢迎的封面文章，进一步提出了更多“长寿热点”，其中包括日本冲绳县和美国加州洛马·林达（Loma Linda）。

paragraphs.en: Buettner trademarked Blue Zones and partnered with Poulain. He went on to write best-selling books, offer cooking classes, charge substantial speaking fees, and found a company that made millions of dollars by issuing municipalities Blue Zone stamps of approval. The aggressive commercialization eventually led to a rupture with Poulain. (According to Science magazine, the researcher ended the partnership after Chanel released a skin-care product called Blue Serum, which was marketed as anti-aging because it included materials from blue zones, and Buettner wanted to sue the luxury label for its use of the term.) Buettner also admitted that he originally included Loma Linda because his editor wanted to identify a blue zone in the U.S., and that he “never bothered to delist it.”
paragraphs.zh: 布特纳将“蓝区”申请了商标，并与普兰合作。他随后出版了畅销书，开设烹饪课程，收取可观的演讲费，还成立了一家公司，通过颁发各市镇“蓝区认证标志”赚取了数百万美元。过于激进的商业化最终与普兰决裂。（据《Science》杂志报道，这位研究者在香奈儿推出一款名为“Blue Serum”的护肤品后终止了合作。该产品以“含有蓝区成分”作为抗衰卖点，而布特纳想以“蓝区”一词的使用问题起诉该奢侈品牌。）布特纳也承认，他最初把洛马·林达纳入清单，是因为编辑要求在美国找一个蓝区案例，而且他“从未费心将其移出名单”。

paragraphs.en: The case of Okinawa, a group of relatively poor islands in the south of Japan, is instructive. Among Japanese prefectures, Okinawa ranks first in consumption of KFC and last in consumption of seafood, sweet potatoes, and leafy greens—its residents have Japan’s highest average body-mass index. More than a third of Okinawan men smoke. Although some books about blue zones encourage people to drink two glasses of red wine a day, “preferably Sardinian Cannonau”—a recommendation plainly at odds with current public-health advice—Okinawans like beer. If we want to live a long time, should we do as the Okinawans do?
paragraphs.zh: 冲绳这个日本南部相对贫困群岛的案例很有启发性。就日本各都道府县而言，冲绳在肯德基消费量上居首位，却在海鲜、红薯和绿叶蔬菜消费上居末位——其居民的平均体质指数（BMI）是日本最高的。超过三分之一的冲绳男性吸烟。尽管一些关于蓝区的书籍鼓励人们每天喝两杯红葡萄酒，‘最好是撒丁岛坎诺内葡萄酒’这一建议明显与当前的公共卫生建议相冲突，但冲绳人偏爱啤酒。要想活得更长，我们应该像冲绳人那样做吗？

paragraphs.en: Probably not. In 2010, Okinawa reportedly had twice as many centenarians per capita as the rest of the country. But, as Poulain himself has pointed out, Okinawa was the site of a devastating American bombing campaign during the Second World War; much of its infrastructure and essentially all its civil records were destroyed. U.S. authorities reconstructed the civil registry by asking survivors to report their age, a process complicated by language barriers and a traditional Japanese calendar that differed from the Gregorian. The country’s postwar welfare system distributed some benefits based on age, and many Okinawans requested that their records be updated. There is a strong correlation between the number of requests made in a village and the supposed life span of its residents.
paragraphs.zh: 很可能不应该。2010 年，据称冲绳每百万人口拥有的百岁老人数量是全国其他地区的两倍。但正如普兰本人所指出的，冲绳是二战期间美国毁灭性轰炸行动的受害地，其基础设施以及几乎全部民政记录被毁。美国当局在重建户籍时，要求幸存者自行申报年龄，但这一过程受到语言障碍和日本传统历法与格里历差异的影响。战后日本的福利制度按年龄发放部分补贴，许多冲绳人因此要求更新自己的年龄记录。一个村庄提出年龄更改申请的数量与该村居民的“预估寿命”之间存在很强的相关性。

paragraphs.en: Our tendency to fetishize faraway lands, trying to unearth their secrets to slow aging, can seem strange when one considers that wealthy Americans already live into their eighties and nineties. Scientists have reliable data for octogenarians and nonagenarians—one research project, the Harvard Study of Adult Development, has been running since 1938—and the lessons are clear enough. The things that help us live longer are things that pretty much everyone wants: money, education, safety, clean air and water, community. Of course, these things are unevenly distributed, and the consequences of that are readily apparent. Research led by the economist Raj Chetty has found that the highest-earning Americans live more than a decade longer than those in the bottom one per cent. And the gap has been widening. Between 2001 and 2014, life expectancy among the top five per cent of earners increased by nearly three years; in the bottom five per cent, it essentially stagnated.
paragraphs.zh: 当我们过度迷恋遥远的地方，试图从那里挖掘延缓衰老的秘密时，这看起来有些怪异，尤其是想到富裕的美国人已经可以活到八十多岁、九十多岁。科学界对于八十岁和九十岁人群有可靠数据——例如从1938年开始持续进行的《哈佛成人发展研究》就是其中之一——并且结论已经相当清楚。帮助我们活得更久的因素，大多是人人都想要的东西：金钱、教育、安全、清洁的空气和水、以及社区。毫无疑问，这些资源分布并不均衡，而且后果一目了然。经济学家拉杰·切蒂（Raj Chetty）的研究发现，最赚钱的美国人比最低百分之一的人群平均多活十多年。这种差距还在扩大。2001年至2014年间，最高收入五个百分点的人群预期寿命几乎增加了三年；而最低五个百分点人群的寿命几乎停滞不前。

paragraphs.en: One hardly needs a list of blue zones to think of commonsense ways to improve life span. We can invest in biomedical research and enact laws and regulations to reduce pollution, design walkable neighborhoods, establish safe public spaces, create affordable housing, and curb smoking. Such strategies might not trend on TikTok, but they would be more effective than the latest juice cleanse. Unfortunately, the pursuit of longevity—like so much else in American life—is increasingly an individualistic endeavor. We adopt new health fads as though they are forms of personal expression, experimenting with cold plunges and red-light therapy, wearable devices and peptide injections. Though it’s nice to think that striving to be healthy is cool or popular—it wasn’t always—the quest for extending life can become monomaniacal, and we easily forget that one’s life span is not, by and large, a life-style choice. Instead, longevity has always been a communal affair.
paragraphs.zh: 几乎不用列出那些“长寿区”名单，我们就能想到一些常识性的办法来延长寿命。我们可以加大生物医学研究投入，并制定法律法规以减少污染、建设步行友好的社区、设立安全的公共空间、提供可负担的住房，以及限制吸烟。这些策略可能不会在TikTok上成为潮流，但它们比最新的果蔬排毒疗法更有效。不幸的是，追求长寿——像美国生活中的许多事情一样——正日益成为一种个人主义化的行为。我们把新的健康潮流当成个人表达方式，在冷水浴、红光疗法、可穿戴设备和肽类注射之间反复试验。尽管把健康生活看成“很酷”或“很时髦”固然令人愉快——而且这并非从前如此——延长生命的追求很容易变成偏执，我们也很容易忘记：一个人的寿命在根本上并不是一种生活方式选择。长寿一直以来都是一件共同的社会事务。

paragraphs.en: In the early eighteen-hundreds, a Belgian astronomer named Adolphe Quetelet coined the term “social physics” to describe a method for modelling human development. Stargazers often made small errors when recording the position of planets; the individual errors were unpredictable, but, in the aggregate, they followed a pattern. We now call this pattern a Gaussian distribution, for the astronomer Carl Friedrich Gauss, or, more commonly, a bell curve. Quetelet showed that similar patterns could be found in social phenomena, including births, deaths, marriage, and crime. There was some variation—and, of course, each year there were different people being born, dying, marrying, and committing crimes—but over-all rates were remarkably stable. These occurrences could not be chalked up to individual choices. They were products of measurable—and modifiable—social conditions. A historian later summarized Quetelet’s thesis: “Society prepares the crime, the criminal commits it.”
paragraphs.zh: 十九世纪早期，名叫阿道夫·凯特莱（Adolphe Quetelet）的比利时天文学家提出了“社会物理学”这一术语，用来描述一种建模人类发展过程的方法。天文爱好者在记录行星位置时常会出现小错误；这些单个错误难以预测，但放在总体上却呈现出规律。我们现在把这种规律称为高斯分布，以天文学家卡尔·弗里德里希·高斯（Carl Friedrich Gauss）命名，或者更常见地称为钟形曲线。凯特莱展示了同样的模式也能在社会现象中被观察到，包括出生、死亡、婚姻和犯罪。确实存在差异，当然每一年出生、死亡、结婚和犯罪的人都是不同的个体，但总体发生率却惊人地稳定。这些现象不能归咎于个人选择。它们是可衡量、也可以改变的社会条件的产物。后来一位历史学家总结了凯特莱的观点：‘社会造就犯罪，犯罪者只是执行者。’

paragraphs.en: Not all of Quetelet’s ideas were good ones; he also contended that a person’s physical traits determined social outcomes. Still, it is unfortunate that today’s longevity discourse has become so fully untethered from his sociological point of view. Influencers who peddle supplements and squats are ignoring the enormous role of societal conditions. During my medical residency, in Boston, I spent some of my time seeing patients from Roxbury, Dorchester, and other low-income neighborhoods, where they were more likely to be exposed to crime, drugs, stress, and toxins such as lead. Other weeks, I’d work at a hospital in Newton, a wealthy suburb a few miles away, where kids grew up in large homes on tree-lined streets. In both places, I could offer patients the same basic advice on healthy habits. But it would be absurd to think that my advice helped some Newton residents live into their nineties while many of my less affluent patients might barely make it to seventy. Society prepares the life span; the individual lives it.
paragraphs.zh: 凯特莱的所有观点并非全是正确的；他也曾主张一个人的体貌特征决定了社会结果。尽管如此，遗憾的是，如今关于长寿的讨论已经与他的社会学观点几乎完全脱节。那些推销补充剂和“深蹲训练”的意见领袖，正在忽视社会条件所起的巨大作用。在我在波士顿的住院医阶段，我有一部分时间服务于来自罗克斯伯里、道奇斯特和其他低收入社区的患者，他们更容易接触到犯罪、毒品、压力，以及铅等毒物。另一些周里，我会在几英里外的富裕郊区牛顿的一家医院值班，那里的孩子在绿树成荫的街道上、宽敞的住房里长大。我在两个地方都能给病人提供相同的健康习惯建议。但要认为我的建议能让一些牛顿居民活到九十多岁，而我的许多经济不宽裕的患者可能连七十岁都很难到达，那是荒谬的。社会决定了寿命长度；个体只是度过自己的生命。

paragraphs.en: Of course, we still have to make decisions about our health. We can’t wait around for governments and institutions—especially this government and these institutions—to bring about the conditions for a wholesome and prosperous society. So what, exactly, should we do? The longevity-industrial complex tends to frame this question in oddly specific ways. Should you eat or avoid eggs? Seek out or shun coffee? Exercise in the morning or the evening? Walk seven thousand steps a day or ten thousand? Nowadays, influencers often invoke scientific research, but it tends to be stripped of context and caveats; they might cite small-animal studies to justify bold recommendations for humans, or argue that everyone can benefit from treatments that are approved for narrow applications. We keep hearing about the new drug that will change our lives. In the two-thousands, David A. Sinclair, a Harvard biologist and a co-author of “Lifespan: Why We Age—and Why We Don’t Have To,” declared that resveratrol, a compound found in grape skins and red wine, was “as close to a miraculous molecule as you can find.” He sold a resveratrol-focussed startup to GlaxoSmithKline. But a researcher at the National Institute on Aging later told the Times that the molecule’s “actual use for humans still needs to be discovered,” and G.S.K. eventually abandoned work on the compound. More recently, in “Outlive: The Science and Art of Longevity,” Peter Attia promoted rapamycin, a molecule that was first sampled on the South Pacific island of Rapa Nui and developed into an immunosuppressant for organ-transplant patients. Laboratory studies have shown that rapamycin could extend the life spans of yeast and mice—but, in a recent randomized human trial, older adults on the medication didn’t seem to experience any physical gains. If anything, they benefitted less from exercise and had more side effects than those who didn’t take the drug; one participant developed a serious infection. Attia and other influencers have said that they have stopped taking the drug, owing to mouth sores, infections, and other complications.
paragraphs.zh: 当然，我们仍然必须对自身健康做出选择。我们不能指望政府和机构——尤其是这个政府和这些机构——先把社会营造得健康而繁荣。那我们究竟该怎么办？长寿产业复合体往往以奇怪而具体的方式提出这个问题。你该不该吃蛋？该不该喝咖啡？应该在早上运动还是晚上运动？每天走七千步好，还是一万步好？如今，网红常常借用科学研究，但这些研究往往脱离了语境和限制条件；他们可能引用小型动物实验来为大胆的人体建议背书，或主张每个人都能从本来只适用于狭窄场景的治疗方案中受益。我们不断听到“将改变我们生活的新药”在被宣传。二十世纪末到二十一世纪初，哈佛生物学家、大作《Lifespan: Why We Age—and Why We Don’t Have To》的合著者 David A. Sinclair 说过，白藜芦醇——一种存在于葡萄皮和红葡萄酒中的化合物——是“你能找到的最接近神奇分子”的物质。他还把一家以白藻芦醇为核心的初创公司卖给了葛兰素史克。后来，美国国家老龄研究所的一位研究人员在《The Times》上表示，这种分子“对人体的实际用途仍有待发现”，而 GSK 最终放弃了对该化合物的研究。最近，在《生命的延续：长寿科学与艺术》一书中，Peter Attia 推广了雷帕霉素，这是一种最早在南太平洋的复活节岛（Rapa Nui）采样、后来被开发为器官移植患者免疫抑制剂的分子。实验室研究显示雷帕霉素能延长酵母和小鼠的寿命，但在一项最近的人体随机试验中，服用该药的老年人似乎没有任何体能收益。更糟的是，他们从运动中获益更少，且副作用比未服药者更多；一名受试者还出现了严重感染。Attia 和其他网红后来都表示，自己已停止服用该药，因为会出现口腔溃疡、感染和其他并发症。

paragraphs.en: A fundamental challenge that longevity gurus face is that what’s true is often boring, and what’s interesting often isn’t true. Against this backdrop, “Eat Your Ice Cream: Six Simple Rules for a Long and Healthy Life” (Norton), by the physician and bioethicist Ezekiel J. Emanuel, arrives as a welcome tonic. The book follows a structure characteristic of the longevity genre: divide the text into a handful of familiar sections, start each chapter with an anecdote, review some relevant research, and conclude with tips on how readers can apply your recommendations. (Move! Sleep! Socialize!) The longevity industry profits from mystifying the obvious, but Emanuel largely dispenses with pretense. He tells us to brush our teeth twice a day and then moves on to such closely held longevity secrets as abstaining from smoking, soda, and drunk driving. These are hardly paradigm-shifting insights, and that’s exactly the point. We already know how to improve our chances of living a long life—exercise, eat well, sleep enough, maintain relationships, don’t drink to excess, don’t smoke at all. (In a notable exception, Emanuel reviews evidence that, weirdly, eating ice cream is linked to a lower risk of diabetes and heart disease—a controversial correlation that gives the book its title.)
paragraphs.zh: 长寿导师面临的一个根本性难题是：真实的往往很枯燥，吸引人的往往并不真实。在这种背景下，医生兼生物伦理学家 Ezekiel J. Emanuel 的《吃掉你的冰激凌：长寿与健康人生的六条简单规则》（Norton）显得格外清醒，像一剂良药。该书遵循了长寿题材常见结构：把内容分成几个熟悉的版块，每章以一个小故事开头，回顾一些相关研究，最后给出读者可执行的建议。（运动！睡眠！社交！）长寿产业靠的是把显而易见的东西复杂化赚钱，但 Emanuel 大体上不做矫饰。他告诉我们要每天刷两次牙，然后转向那些几乎被奉为“长寿秘诀”的事项，比如戒烟、戒含糖汽水和避免酒后驾车。这些都不是颠覆性见解，恰恰正是重点。我们早就知道如何提高长寿概率——运动、饮食健康、保证足够睡眠、维持人际关系、不过量饮酒、不要吸烟。（值得一提的是，Emanuel 在书中还回顾了证据，指出奇怪的是，吃冰激凌与较低的糖尿病和心脏病风险相关联；这是一项有争议的相关性，也是书名的来源。）

paragraphs.en: Emanuel makes the case that the quest for a meaningful life is more important than the quest for a lengthy one. “Wellness and living long are only means,” he writes. “They are not, themselves, the essence of a good life.” Sometimes the means even get in the way. If I skip dinner at a friend’s house because it’s going to ding my sleep score, or if I totally abstain from my favorite food because it’ll mess up the stats on my continuous glucose monitor, my life might be worse, not better. As I read Emanuel’s book, I started to think of the pursuit of longevity as akin to the pursuit of happiness. John Stuart Mill famously argued that people don’t find happiness by furiously chasing it—they find it by seeking something other than their happiness. “Aiming thus at something else, they find happiness by the way,” Mill wrote. The brevity of “Eat Your Ice Cream” is itself a kind of advice. Absorb the basics, Emanuel seems to be saying, and then get on with your life. He wants to help us make healthy choices, but he also wants us to see that even the best habits can’t stop us from aging. The first rule of life, Emanuel writes, is that we’re all going to die. ♦
paragraphs.zh: Emanuel 认为，比起追求长寿，更重要的是追求有意义的生命。“健康和长寿只是手段，”他写道，“它们本身并不是美好人生的本质。”有时这些手段反而会成为阻碍。如果我因为担心影响睡眠评分而在朋友家不吃晚餐，或者因为担心连续葡萄糖监测器上的数据被我最爱的食物弄乱而完全不吃它，那么我的生活可能变得更糟，而不是更好。读完 Emanuel 的书后，我开始把追求长寿类比为追求幸福。约翰·斯图尔特·密尔曾著名地指出，人们并不是通过拼命追逐幸福来获得幸福——人们是在追求别的事情时顺势获得幸福。“当人们因此而追求其他目标时，他们反而顺便找到了幸福，”密尔写道。《吃掉你的冰激凌》篇幅短小，本身就是一种建议。Emanuel 似乎在说，先吸收那些基础内容，然后继续你的生活。他想帮助我们做出健康选择，但也想让我们看到，即便是最好的习惯也不能阻止我们老去。Emanuel 写道，人生第一条规则就是：我们都会死。♦
